content:
# Judges Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_275.jpg, JDG_page_276.jpg

1. Then Jerubbaal (that is, Gideon) and all the people who were with him rose early and encamped beside the spring of Harod. And the camp of Midian was north of them, by the hill of Moreh, in the valley.

2. The LORD said to Gideon, "The people with you are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hand, lest Israel boast over me, saying, 'My own hand has saved me.'

3. Now therefore proclaim in the ears of the people, saying, 'Whoever is fearful and trembling, let him return home and depart from Mount Gilead.'" Then 22,000 of the people returned, and 10,000 remained.

4. And the LORD said to Gideon, "The people are still too many. Take them down to the water, and I will test them for you there, and anyone of whom I say to you, 'This one shall go with you,' shall go with you, and anyone of whom I say to you, 'This one shall not go with you,' shall not go."

5. So he brought the people down to the water. And the LORD said to Gideon, "Every one who laps the water with his tongue, as a dog laps, you shall set by himself. Likewise, every one who kneels down to drink."

6. And the number of those who lapped, putting their hands to their mouths, was 300 men, but all the rest of the people knelt down to drink water.

7. And the LORD said to Gideon, "With the 300 men who lapped I will save you and give the Midianites into your hand, and let all the others go every man to his home."

8. So the people took provisions in their hands, and their trumpets. And he sent all the rest of Israel every man to his tent, but retained the 300 men. And the camp of Midian was below him in the valley.

9. That same night the LORD said to him, "Arise, go down against the camp, for I have given it into your hand.

10. But if you are afraid to go down, go down to the camp with Purah your servant.

11. And you shall hear what they say, and afterward your hands shall be strengthened to go down against the camp." Then he went down with Purah his servant to the outposts of the armed men who were in the camp.

12. And the Midianites and the Amalekites and all the people of the east lay along the valley like locusts in abundance, and their camels were without number, as the sand that is on the seashore in abundance.

13. When Gideon came, behold, a man was telling a dream to his companion. He said, "Behold, I dreamed a dream, and behold, a cake of barley bread tumbled into the camp of Midian and came to the tent and struck it so that it fell and turned it upside down, so that the tent lay flat."

14. And his companion answered, "This is no other than the sword of Gideon the son of Joash, a man of Israel; God has given into his hand Midian and all the camp."

15. As soon as Gideon heard the telling of the dream and its interpretation, he worshiped. And he returned to the camp of Israel and said, "Arise, for the LORD has given the camp of Midian into your hand."

16. And he divided the 300 men into three companies and put trumpets into the hands of all of them and empty jars, with torches inside the jars.

17. And he said to them, "Look at me, and do likewise. When I come to the outskirts of the camp, do as I do.

18. When I blow the trumpet, I and all who are with me, then blow the trumpets also on every side of all the camp and shout, 'For the LORD and for Gideon!'"

19. So Gideon and the hundred men who were with him came to the outskirts of the camp at the beginning of the middle watch, just after they had set the guard. And they blew the trumpets and smashed the jars that were in their hands.

20. Then the three companies blew the trumpets and broke the jars. They held in their left hands the torches, and in their right hands the trumpets to blow. And they cried out, "A sword for the LORD and for Gideon!"

21. Every man stood in his place around the camp, and all the army ran. They cried out and fled.

22. When they blew the 300 trumpets, the LORD set every man's sword against his companion and against all the army. And the army fled as far as Beth-shittah toward Zererah, as far as the border of Abel-meholah, by Tabbath.

23. And the men of Israel were called out from Naphtali and from Asher and from all Manasseh, and they pursued after the Midianites.

24. Gideon sent messengers throughout all the hill country of Ephraim, saying, "Come down against the Midianites and capture the waters against them, as far as Beth-barah, and also the Jordan." So all the men of Ephraim were called out, and they captured the waters as far as Beth-barah, and also the Jordan.

25. And they captured the two princes of Midian, Oreb and Zeeb. They killed Oreb at the rock of Oreb, and Zeeb they killed at the winepress of Zeeb. They pursued Midian, and they brought the heads of Oreb and Zeeb to Gideon across the Jordan.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 7 is the military climax of the Gideon narrative and one of the most famous stories in the Bible. The deliberate reduction from 32,000 to 300 serves a single theological purpose: God alone gets the credit (v.2). The night attack — with trumpets, jars, and torches instead of weapons — makes the point concrete: Israel's "army" has no swords. The only sword is God's (v.22).

**The structure:** The chapter moves in three acts: (1) the double reduction of the army (vv.1-8), (2) the espionage and the barley-bread dream (vv.9-15), and (3) the night attack and rout (vv.16-25). Each act intensifies the theme of divine sufficiency and human inadequacy.

**Oreb and Zeeb (v.25):** The names mean "Raven" and "Wolf" — animal names for Midianite commanders. Their deaths at "the rock of Oreb" and "the winepress of Zeeb" give these places their names. Isaiah 10:26 later references this battle.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.2 — "boast over me" vs. "boast to me"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "glorify itself against me" | yithpa'er alai — reflexive hithpa'el |
| LOGOS | "boast over me" | alai as "over/against" |
| PNEUMA | "boast to me that they saved themselves" | Explanatory expansion |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew yithpa'er alai (hithpa'el of pa'ar + al) means "to glorify oneself against/at the expense of." EMET's "glorify itself against me" is precise but awkward. LOGOS's "boast over me" captures the sense — Israel claiming credit that belongs to God. PNEUMA adds an explanatory clause ("that they saved themselves") that is already implied by the following quotation ("My own hand has saved me").

**Status:** Minor. PNEUMA adds unnecessary expansion.

---

### v.8 — "trumpets" vs. "shofars" vs. "rams' horns"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "shofars" | shofarot — ram's horn trumpets |
| LOGOS | "trumpets" | Standard English rendering |
| PNEUMA | "rams' horns" | Descriptive |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew shofar is a specific instrument — a ram's horn used for signaling, not a metallic trumpet. However, "trumpets" is the standard English rendering in formal Bible translation and avoids the need for repeated explanatory notes. EMET's transliteration "shofars" preserves the Hebrew term but may be unfamiliar. PNEUMA's "rams' horns" is descriptive but loses the sense of a specific, named instrument.

**Status:** Minor. Recurring instrument terminology.

---

### v.15 — "he worshiped" vs. "he bowed down" vs. "he bowed in worship before the LORD"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he bowed down" | vayyishtachu — he prostrated himself |
| LOGOS | "he worshiped" | Standard rendering of shachah |
| PNEUMA | "he bowed in worship before the LORD" | Combined + adds "before the LORD" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew vayyishtachu (from shachah, "to bow/prostrate/worship") simply says "he bowed down" — the direction (toward God) is implicit. PNEUMA adds "before the LORD" which is not in the Hebrew text. This is a characteristic PNEUMA expansion: making explicit what the original leaves implicit. The text's restraint — Gideon simply bows — is itself powerful.

**Status:** Minor. PNEUMA adds to the text.

---

### v.19 — "the beginning of the middle watch" vs. "just after midnight"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the beginning of the middle watch" | rosh ha'ashmoret hattikhonh |
| LOGOS | "the beginning of the middle watch" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "just after midnight" | Converts to modern time |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Hebrew describes the Israelite three-watch night system (evening watch, middle watch, morning watch). The "beginning of the middle watch" corresponds roughly to 10 PM, not midnight (which would be the middle of the middle watch). PNEUMA's "just after midnight" is both an inaccurate conversion and a loss of the ancient time-keeping system that adds historical texture.

**Status:** Moderate. Inaccurate time conversion.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA adds to the text | 2 | Explanatory expansion (v.2), "before the LORD" (v.15) |
| PNEUMA converts cultural reference | 1 | "Middle watch" → "midnight" (v.19, inaccurate) |
| Instrument terminology | 1 | shofar rendering (v.8) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 7's disagreements are relatively minor. The most consequential is v.19 — PNEUMA's conversion of the middle watch to "midnight" is not only a loss of cultural texture but factually inaccurate. The broader pattern continues: PNEUMA tends to explain what LOGOS and EMET let stand, adding clarity at the cost of the text's own restraint.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
